advantages: Provides a unified language for PMF/PDF models, ML losses, MLE, and Bayes—one object L(θ) links theory to practice.
Log-likelihood turns products into sums, enabling gradient-based optimization at scale on mini-batches.
Relative likelihood values compare parameter settings without needing a probability distribution over θ.
Factorizes cleanly under independence, so per-example losses in deep learning are exact log-likelihood contributions.
disadvantages: Likelihood can be arbitrarily small for large n (product of many factors), causing underflow unless you work in log space.
A high likelihood under a misspecified model still fits the wrong story—L(θ) is only as good as the chosen f(x | θ).
Boundary maxima (e.g. p = 0 or 1 with small samples) and non-identifiability can make raw argmax L(θ) unstable without regularization.
Likelihood alone does not quantify uncertainty over θ; you need Fisher information, bootstrap, or a Bayesian posterior for that.
